stem: Let \( S = \left\{\, [[a]] - \dfrac{[[b]]}{n} \;:\; n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \,\right\} \subseteq \mathbb{R} \). What is \( \sup S \), the least upper bound of \( S \)?
solution: Since \( b = [[b]] > 0 \), the terms \( [[a]] - \dfrac{[[b]]}{n} \) strictly increase toward \( [[a]] \) and never reach it. Thus \( [[a]] \) is an upper bound and every smaller number is exceeded, so \( \sup S = [[answer]] \) (a supremum not attained by any element).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \min S = \inf S \), the value at \( n = 1 \), not the supremum.
This flips the sign of the correction term, overshooting the true bound.
This is the \( n = 1 \) term shifted by another \( b \), well below the supremum.
This is one more than the correct supremum, so it is not the least upper bound.
This is one less than the correct supremum; some elements exceed it, so it is not an upper bound.
This is two more than the correct supremum, not the least such bound.

stem: Let \( S = \left\{\, \dfrac{[[A]]n}{n + [[B]]} \;:\; n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \,\right\} \subseteq \mathbb{R} \). What is \( \sup S \)?
solution: Rewrite \( \dfrac{[[A]]n}{n+[[B]]} = [[A]] - \dfrac{[[A]]\cdot [[B]]}{n+[[B]]} \). As \( n \to \infty \) the subtracted term shrinks to \( 0 \), and it is always positive, so the terms increase strictly toward \( [[A]] \) without ever reaching it. Hence \( \sup S = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the \( n=1 \) term, the minimum of \( S \), not its supremum.
This adds the coefficient and the shift instead of taking the limiting ratio.
This is one less than the correct supremum.
This is one more than the correct supremum, so it is not a least upper bound.
This divides by \( [[B]] \) instead of recognizing the limiting ratio is just \( [[A]] \).
This subtracts instead of recognizing the shift term vanishes in the limit.

stem: Let \( S = \left\{\, [[a]] + (-1)^{n}\dfrac{[[b]]}{n} \;:\; n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \,\right\} \subseteq \mathbb{R} \). What is \( \sup S \)?
solution: For even \( n \), the terms are \( [[a]] + [[b]]/n \), decreasing toward \( [[a]] \) as \( n \) grows, so their largest value is at \( n=2 \): \( [[a]] + [[b]]/2 \). For odd \( n \), the terms are \( [[a]] - [[b]]/n < [[a]] \), all below every even term. So the maximum of the whole set — attained at \( n=2 \) — is \( \sup S = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \inf S \) (the odd-\( n \) minimum at \( n=1 \)), not the supremum.
This is the common limit \( [[a]] \) that both parities approach, but the even terms exceed it, so it is not an upper bound.
This uses the \( n=1 \) even-style correction instead of the true \( n=2 \) maximum.
This uses the \( n=4 \) term, which is smaller than the true maximum at \( n=2 \).
This is one more than the correct supremum.
This is one less than the correct supremum.

stem: Let \( S = \left\{\, [[a]]n^{2} - [[b]]n \;:\; n = 1, 2, \ldots, [[N]] \,\right\} \), a FINITE set of \( [[N]] \) real numbers. What is \( \inf S \) (equivalently, \( \min S \))?
solution: Since \( S \) is finite, its infimum is attained: it is the smallest value of \( f(n) = [[a]]n^2 - [[b]]n \) over integers \( 1 \le n \le [[N]] \). Treating \( f \) as a parabola opening upward, its vertex is at \( n = \dfrac{[[b]]}{2\cdot [[a]]} = [[nstar]] \), an integer inside the range. So \( \inf S = f([[nstar]]) = [[a]]\cdot [[nstar]]^{2} - [[b]]\cdot [[nstar]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the value at \( n=1 \), an endpoint, not the true minimum at the parabola's vertex.
This is the value at \( n=[[N]] \), the other endpoint, which is larger since the parabola rises away from the vertex.
This is the value one step to the right of the vertex, not at the vertex itself.
This is the value one step to the left of the vertex, not at the vertex itself.
This is one more than the correct minimum.
This is one less than the correct minimum.

stem: Let \( S = \left\{\, \dfrac{[[A]]}{n} \;:\; n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \,\right\} \cup \left\{\, [[B]] - \dfrac{[[C]]}{n} \;:\; n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \,\right\} \subseteq \mathbb{R} \). What is \( \sup S \)?
solution: The first family \( [[A]]/n \) is decreasing, so its largest value (attained at \( n=1 \)) is \( [[A]] \), and it is a valid candidate upper bound only for that piece. The second family \( [[B]] - [[C]]/n \) increases toward \( [[B]] \) without reaching it. So \( \sup S \) is whichever of \( [[A]] \) and \( [[B]] \) is larger: \( \sup S = \max([[A]], [[B]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This computes \( \min([[A]],[[B]]) \) instead of the maximum.
This only considers the first family's bound, ignoring whether the second family's limit is larger.
This only considers the second family's limiting bound, ignoring whether the first family's maximum is larger.
This adds the two candidate bounds instead of taking the larger one.
This averages the two candidate bounds instead of taking the larger one.
This is just the gap between the two candidates, not the supremum itself.
